title: SEGMENT 5 — The multi-gene-edit pig: subtractions and additions

lines.text: SEGMENT 5 — The multi-gene-edit pig: subtractions and additions
Let me describe the modern xeno pig in detail.
I want you to see it not as a generic "GMO pig" but as a very specifically designed biological device.
There are roughly a dozen edits in the Revivicor 10-gene pig that David Bennett received, and there are nearly seventy in the eGenesis pigs that the 2024 kidney patients received.
Each edit has a reason.

The edits fall into three families: subtractions (knocking out pig genes that make foreign antigens), subtractions (knocking out porcine retroviruses, which I'll save for the next segment), and additions (inserting human genes that calm down the human immune system once the organ is in place).
Let's go through them.
First family of edits, the antigen knockouts.
We've already talked about GalT — alpha-gal, gone.
Then CMAH — Neu5Gc, gone.
Then β4GalNT2 — Sda, gone.
That's the triple knockout, the GMO patient I want you to remember: GalT, CMAH, β4GalNT2 — three sugar-modifying enzymes deleted, three xenoantigens stripped off the pig's cell surface.
With just these three knockouts, when you take a sample of human blood and pour it over pig cells in a dish, the human antibodies barely stick at all.
The chemical foreignness of the cell surface has been radically reduced.
Second family, the human gene insertions.
Even with the antigens reduced, the human immune system has other layers of attack.
There's the complement cascade I told you about — the molecular drill that punches holes in foreign cells.
To suppress that, you insert genes encoding human complement regulatory proteins.
The three big ones are CD46 — which has a clinical name, membrane cofactor protein; CD55 — also called decay-accelerating factor; and CD59 — the membrane attack complex inhibitor.
In a normal human, these three proteins sit on the surface of all your cells, telling complement: "this is one of us, don't drill us.
" Pigs make their own versions of these regulators, but the pig versions are tuned for pig complement, and they don't recognize human complement well.
So we insert the human versions.
Now when human complement starts to activate on the pig endothelium, the human regulators sitting there shut the cascade off.
The complement attack stops before it can really get going.
There's also a clotting problem.
When pig endothelium meets human blood, even without the antibody attack, you tend to get inappropriate clotting — because human clotting proteins and pig anticoagulant proteins don't mesh well.
To fix this, you insert human anticoagulant genes like thrombomodulin and endothelial protein C receptor, which keep the pig endothelium from triggering human clots.
And finally there's a slower immune layer — the T cells.
T cells take days to ramp up, but they're powerful.
Once they decide an organ is foreign, they attack.

To slow T cells down, you can insert genes that make the pig endothelium less stimulating to human T cells — genes like CD47, the "don't eat me" signal, in a human form; or genes that downregulate the major histocompatibility complex on the pig surface.
Add it all up, and you have the modern multi-edit pig.
The triple knockout strips off the sugars.
The complement regulator insertions stop the membrane attack complex.
The anticoagulant insertions stop the clotting.
The T cell modulators slow the cellular response.
Each layer of the immune system has been individually disarmed, by a specific edit, for a specific reason.
It's the GMO patient — and I want you to hold that phrase, because it's the right framing.
The pig is not just a donor.
The pig is a custom-engineered biological device, designed from the genome up, to look as little like a foreign object as we can make it.

The Revivicor 10-edit pig has the triple knockout, two complement regulator transgenes (CD46 and CD55), two anticoagulant transgenes, and a couple of others including a growth hormone receptor knockout to keep the heart from growing too big after transplant.
The eGenesis 69-edit pig adds more antigen knockouts, more transgenes, and a sweep of edits across the porcine endogenous retroviruses, which I'll cover in the next segment.
The trajectory of the field is: more edits, more layers of immune evasion, more compatibility with the human environment.
If you want one mental image to take away from this segment, here it is.
The unedited pig is a foreigner who walks into your country wearing a different uniform, speaking a different language, with a different passport.
The edited pig has been given local clothes, taught the local language, and issued a human passport.
The immune system still notices small differences, sometimes — but it doesn't immediately call the police.
That analogy is useful but incomplete, because there is one more class of problem, and it isn't about appearance.
It's about hitchhikers.
That's the next segment, and it's its own kind of horror story.
